title: AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable
tex_block_html: If $G$ is acyclic then all non-colliders are blockable.

tex_statement.raw:
% Claim statement file for row claim_3_20 -- "AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solve_chapter.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker it dispatches) fills the body below with the
% claim's statement(s) from the lecture notes -- statement only, no proof
% (proofs live in the sibling `claim_3_20_proof_*.tex` file).
%
% This file is a sibling of `main.tex` inside `<subsection>/tex/`. The
% `subfiles` package lets it render both standalone and as part of
% `main.tex`. Note: `main.tex` does NOT \subfile this statement file -- the
% sibling `_proof_` file restates the statement above the proof, so
% including both in `main.tex` would be redundant. This file exists for
% standalone reading / grep convenience.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_20 (statement)
% title: AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_20}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_20}

\begin{Rem}[AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable]
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}) and suppose $G$ is acyclic in the sense of def \ref{def-acylic}. Then, for every integer $n \ge 0$, every walk
    \[
        \pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n)
    \]
    in $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def:walks}, item~i.\ (so $v_0, \dots, v_n \in J \cup V$, $a_0, \dots, a_{n-1} \in E \cup L$, and the walk constraint
    \[
        \bigl(a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \;\land\; a_k \in E \cup L\bigr)
        \;\lor\;
        \bigl(a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \;\land\; a_k \in E\bigr)
    \]
    holds at every $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}$), and every position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$, the following implication holds:
    \[
        \bigl(\text{$k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$ per def \ref{def:collider_noncollider}, item~i.}\bigr)
        \;\implies\;
        \bigl(\text{$k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ per def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider}}\bigr).
    \]
\end{Rem}

\end{document}

Then, for every integer $n \ge 0$, every walk \[ \pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n) \] in $G$ in the sense of def def:walks, item i.\ (so $v_0, \dots, v_n \in J \cup V$, $a_0, \dots, a_{n-1} \in E \cup L$, and the walk constraint \[ \bigl(a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \;\land\; a_k \in E \cup L\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl(a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \;\land\; a_k \in E\bigr) \] holds at every $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}$), and every position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$, the following implication holds: \[ \bigl(\text{$k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$ per def \ref{def:collider_noncollider}, item~i.}\bigr) \;\implies\; \bigl(\text{$k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ per def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider}}\bigr). \]

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_20 (proof, with statement restated for readability)
% title: AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_20}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_20}

% --- statement (restated, synced verbatim from
% --- claim_3_20_statement_AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable.tex) ----------------
\begin{Rem}[AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable]
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}) and suppose $G$ is acyclic in the sense of def \ref{def-acylic}. Then, for every integer $n \ge 0$, every walk
    \[
        \pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n)
    \]
    in $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def:walks}, item~i.\ (so $v_0, \dots, v_n \in J \cup V$, $a_0, \dots, a_{n-1} \in E \cup L$, and the walk constraint
    \[
        \bigl(a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \;\land\; a_k \in E \cup L\bigr)
        \;\lor\;
        \bigl(a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \;\land\; a_k \in E\bigr)
    \]
    holds at every $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}$), and every position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$, the following implication holds:
    \[
        \bigl(\text{$k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$ per def \ref{def:collider_noncollider}, item~i.}\bigr)
        \;\implies\;
        \bigl(\text{$k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ per def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider}}\bigr).
    \]
\end{Rem}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{proof}
% Guided by the LN author's commented-out sketch at
% lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex:1599-1614, adapted to the
% def_3_16 framing of this formalization: the LN sketch's conditions
% "v_k \notin \Sc^G(v_{k \pm 1})" are rewritten as
% "v_{k \pm 1} \in \Sc^G(v_k)" per def_3_16, item iii. The walk-edge
% writing, the end-position branch, and the "concatenation yields a
% directed walk of length \ge 1" step are spelled out explicitly so the
% proof file is self-contained.
Fix $n \ge 0$, a walk $\pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ in $G$ (def \ref{def:walks}, item~i.), and a position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$. Suppose $k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$ (def \ref{def:collider_noncollider}, item~i.). We must show $k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ in the sense of def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider}, namely:
\begin{itemize}
    \item[(B1)] $k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$ in the sense of def \ref{def:collider_noncollider}, item~i.; AND
    \item[(B2)] $k$ is not an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$ in the sense of def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider}.
\end{itemize}
Clause (B1) is the hypothesis. It remains to establish clause (B2).

We split on the position $k$ along $\pi$.

\medskip\noindent\textbf{Case 1 -- end-position ($k = 0$ or $k = n$).}
By def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider}, clause~ii., any unblockable non-collider on $\pi$ must be \emph{interior}, i.e.\ $k \notin \{0, n\}$ (equivalently $1 \le k \le n - 1$). The hypothesis $k \in \{0, n\}$ of this case directly negates clause~ii.\ of def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider}. Therefore $k$ is not an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$, establishing (B2). Hence $k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ in this case.

\medskip\noindent\textbf{Case 2 -- interior position ($1 \le k \le n - 1$).}
We argue by contradiction: suppose, toward contradiction, that $k$ \emph{is} an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$ in the sense of def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider}. We will derive a non-trivial directed walk from $v_k$ to itself in $G$, contradicting def \ref{def-acylic}.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Step 2.1 -- the non-collider hypothesis yields an outgoing walk-edge of $v_k$.}
Since $k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$, def \ref{def:collider_noncollider}, item~i.\ gives $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \le 1$, where
\[
    \mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) = \Bigl|\Bigl\{\, i \in \{k - 1, k\} \,\Bigm|\, 0 \le i \le n - 1 \,\land\, a_i \text{ is an edge into } v_k \text{ in } G \,\Bigr\}\Bigr|.
\]
Since $1 \le k \le n - 1$, both indices $i \in \{k - 1, k\}$ are admissible (they satisfy $0 \le i \le n - 1$), so the index set defining $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$ is $\{k - 1, k\}$ and $|\{k - 1, k\}| = 2$. Hence $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \le 1$ means at least one of the two walk-incident edges $a_{k - 1}, a_k$ is \emph{not} an edge into $v_k$ in the sense of def \ref{def-edge-relations}, item~ii.

We expand what ``not an edge into $v_k$'' forces on each walk-incident edge using the walk constraint of def \ref{def:walks}, item~i.\ (recalled in the statement above). For each admissible index $i \in \{k - 1, k\}$, the walk constraint at $i$ states
\[
    \bigl(a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \;\land\; a_i \in E \cup L\bigr)
        \;\lor\;
    \bigl(a_i = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \;\land\; a_i \in E\bigr),
\]
so $a_i$ is one of three configurations:
\begin{itemize}
    \item[(C1)] $a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$ (a directed edge from $v_i$ to $v_{i+1}$);
    \item[(C2)] $a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in L$ (a bidirected edge with the ordered-pair writing $(v_i, v_{i+1})$);
    \item[(C3)] $a_i = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \in E$ (a directed edge from $v_{i+1}$ to $v_i$).
\end{itemize}
Applying the ``edge into $v_k$'' predicate of def \ref{def-edge-relations}, item~ii.\ ($a_i \in E$ with second coordinate $v_k$, or $a_i \in L$ with either coordinate $v_k$) to each configuration at slot $i \in \{k - 1, k\}$ (so the writing $a_i = (e_1, e_2)$ takes $e_1, e_2 \in \{v_i, v_{i+1}\}$ in some order, and $v_k \in \{v_i, v_{i+1}\}$ because $i \in \{k - 1, k\}$ means $\{v_i, v_{i+1}\}$ contains $v_k$):
\begin{itemize}
    \item For \emph{slot $i = k - 1$} ($v_i = v_{k - 1}, v_{i+1} = v_k$):
    \begin{itemize}
        \item (C1) $a_{k-1} = (v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E$: into $v_k$ (the $E$-clause fires, $e_2 = v_k$).
        \item (C2) $a_{k-1} = (v_{k-1}, v_k) \in L$: into $v_k$ (the $L$-clause fires, $e_2 = v_k$).
        \item (C3) $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$: \emph{not} into $v_k$ (the $E$-clause requires $e_2 = v_k$, but $e_2 = v_{k-1}$; and $L$-clause is excluded since $a_{k-1} \in E$). This is the only configuration at slot $k - 1$ for which $a_{k - 1}$ fails the ``edge into $v_k$'' predicate.
    \end{itemize}
    \item For \emph{slot $i = k$} ($v_i = v_k, v_{i+1} = v_{k+1}$):
    \begin{itemize}
        \item (C1) $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$: \emph{not} into $v_k$ (the $E$-clause requires $e_2 = v_k$, but $e_2 = v_{k+1}$; and $L$-clause is excluded since $a_k \in E$). This is the only configuration at slot $k$ for which $a_k$ fails the ``edge into $v_k$'' predicate.
        \item (C2) $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in L$: into $v_k$ (the $L$-clause fires, $e_1 = v_k$).
        \item (C3) $a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \in E$: into $v_k$ (the $E$-clause fires, $e_2 = v_k$).
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}
In summary, an admissible slot $i \in \{k - 1, k\}$ has $a_i$ \emph{not} an edge into $v_k$ \emph{iff} either
\[
    i = k - 1 \;\text{and}\; a_{k - 1} = (v_k, v_{k - 1}) \in E,
    \qquad\text{or}\qquad
    i = k \;\text{and}\; a_k = (v_k, v_{k + 1}) \in E.
\]
By the previous paragraph, the non-collider hypothesis forces at least one such slot to occur. We capture this in a single statement: there exists a slot $i^* \in \{k - 1, k\}$ at which $a_{i^*}$ is an \emph{outgoing walk-edge of $v_k$ at position $k$ on $\pi$} in the sense of def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider} (the predicate ``$a_i \in E \land e_1 = v_k$''), with the two sub-cases:
\begin{itemize}
    \item[(O1)] $i^* = k - 1$ with $a_{k - 1} = (v_k, v_{k - 1}) \in E$ -- the other endpoint of $a_{k - 1}$ along $\pi$ is $v_{k - 1}$;
    \item[(O2)] $i^* = k$ with $a_k = (v_k, v_{k + 1}) \in E$ -- the other endpoint of $a_k$ along $\pi$ is $v_{k + 1}$.
\end{itemize}
(Both sub-cases may simultaneously hold -- corresponding to the ``fork'' pattern -- but for the argument below we just need at least one.)

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Step 2.2 -- the unblockable assumption forces the other endpoint into $\Sc^G(v_k)$.}
By the contradiction hypothesis, $k$ is unblockable on $\pi$, so clause~iii.\ of def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider} fires:
\[
    \bigl(a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E \;\implies\; v_{k-1} \in \Sc^G(v_k)\bigr)
        \;\land\;
    \bigl(a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E \;\implies\; v_{k+1} \in \Sc^G(v_k)\bigr).
\]
Pick the slot $i^*$ from Step~2.1 and write $w$ for the other walk-endpoint at that slot:
\[
    w := v_{k - 1} \text{ if } i^* = k - 1, \qquad w := v_{k + 1} \text{ if } i^* = k.
\]
In either sub-case the antecedent of the corresponding conjunct fires (by the very definition of $i^*$ in Step~2.1), and we conclude
\[
    w \in \Sc^G(v_k),
\]
together with the directed-edge fact $(v_k, w) \in E$ recorded in Step~2.1.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Step 2.3 -- $w \in \Sc^G(v_k)$ supplies a directed walk from $w$ to $v_k$.}
By def \ref{def_3_5}, item~vii.,
\[
    \Sc^G(v_k) \;=\; \Anc^G(v_k) \,\cap\, \Desc^G(v_k),
\]
so $w \in \Sc^G(v_k)$ entails $w \in \Anc^G(v_k)$. By def \ref{def_3_5}, item~iv., $w \in \Anc^G(v_k)$ means there exists a directed walk $\rho$ from $w$ to $v_k$ in $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def:walks}, item~ii.\ -- a tuple
\[
    \rho \;=\; (w = u_0, b_0, u_1, b_1, \dots, u_{m-1}, b_{m-1}, u_m = v_k)
\]
for some $m \ge 0$, with each $b_j = (u_j, u_{j+1}) \in E$ pointing forward along $\rho$ from $u_j$ to $u_{j+1}$.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Step 2.4 -- concatenating the single edge $(v_k, w) \in E$ with $\rho$ yields a non-trivial directed walk from $v_k$ to itself.}
We assemble the directed walk
\[
    \widetilde{\rho} \;:=\; (v_k, \;(v_k, w), \;w = u_0, \;b_0, \;u_1, \;b_1, \;\dots, \;u_{m-1}, \;b_{m-1}, \;u_m = v_k).
\]
That is, prepend the node $v_k$ and the edge $(v_k, w) \in E$ to $\rho$. We verify that $\widetilde{\rho}$ is a directed walk from $v_k$ to itself in the sense of def \ref{def:walks}, item~ii.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \emph{Node and edge typing.} The nodes of $\widetilde{\rho}$ are $v_k, w = u_0, u_1, \dots, u_m = v_k$, all in $J \cup V$ ($v_k$ by hypothesis of the walk $\pi$, the $u_j$ by the directed-walk $\rho$). The edges are $(v_k, w)$ followed by $b_0, b_1, \dots, b_{m-1}$, all in $E$ ($(v_k, w) \in E$ from Step~2.1, the $b_j \in E$ from $\rho$).
    \item \emph{Length.} The length of $\widetilde{\rho}$, counted by the number of edges, is $1 + m$, where $m \ge 0$. Hence $1 + m \ge 1$, so $\widetilde{\rho}$ is a non-trivial walk (its length is at least~$1$, i.e.\ it has at least one edge).
    \item \emph{Endpoints.} The first node of $\widetilde{\rho}$ is $v_k$ (by construction), and the last node is $u_m = v_k$ (the terminus of $\rho$). So $\widetilde{\rho}$ starts and ends at $v_k$.
    \item \emph{Directed-walk shape.} Per def \ref{def:walks}, item~ii., a directed walk has all its edges of the form ``forward'' $(u_j, u_{j+1}) \in E$ at each successive slot, with arrowheads consistently pointing forward. The first edge of $\widetilde{\rho}$ is $(v_k, w) \in E$, fitting the slot writing $(\text{first node}, \text{second node}) = (v_k, w)$ in $E$; the subsequent edges $b_0, b_1, \dots, b_{m-1}$ are the directed-walk edges of $\rho$, hence fit the same forward-slot writing relative to $\rho$'s nodes; and the join between $(v_k, w)$ and $\rho$ at the node $w = u_0$ is consistent (the head of $(v_k, w)$ equals the start of $\rho$). All arrowheads point forward (from $v_k$ to $w$, then from $u_0$ through to $u_m$); no arrowhead points backward. Hence $\widetilde{\rho}$ has the directed-walk shape of def \ref{def:walks}, item~ii.
\end{itemize}
Therefore $\widetilde{\rho}$ is a directed walk from $v_k$ to $v_k$ in $G$ of length at least~$1$ -- equivalently, a \emph{non-trivial} directed walk from $v_k$ to itself in $G$.

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Step 2.5 -- contradiction with acyclicity.}
By def \ref{def-acylic}, $G$ acyclic asserts that no non-trivial directed walk from any node to itself exists in $G$. But Step~2.4 has constructed exactly such a walk $\widetilde{\rho}$ at the node $v_k \in J \cup V$. Contradiction.

\smallskip
Hence the assumption that $k$ is an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$ is false. So $k$ is not an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$, establishing (B2) in the interior case.

\medskip
In both cases (end-position and interior), the hypothesis ``$k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$'' implies $k$ is not an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$. Combining with (B1), $k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ in the sense of def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider}.
\end{proof}

\end{document}
tex_proof.html:
Remark — AcyclicNonCollidersBlockable

Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg) and suppose $G$ is acyclic in the sense of def def-acylic. Then, for every integer $n \ge 0$, every walk \[ \pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n) \] in $G$ in the sense of def def:walks, item i.\ (so $v_0, \dots, v_n \in J \cup V$, $a_0, \dots, a_{n-1} \in E \cup L$, and the walk constraint \[ \bigl(a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \;\land\; a_k \in E \cup L\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl(a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \;\land\; a_k \in E\bigr) \] holds at every $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}$), and every position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$, the following implication holds: \[ \bigl(\text{$k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$ per def \ref{def:collider_noncollider}, item~i.}\bigr) \;\implies\; \bigl(\text{$k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ per def \ref{def:unblockable_noncollider}}\bigr). \]

Proof.

Fix $n \ge 0$, a walk $\pi = (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ in $G$ (def def:walks, item i.), and a position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$. Suppose $k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$ (def def:collider_noncollider, item i.). We must show $k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ in the sense of def def:unblockable_noncollider, namely:

  • [(B1)] $k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$ in the sense of def def:collider_noncollider, item i.; AND
  • [(B2)] $k$ is not an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$ in the sense of def def:unblockable_noncollider.

Clause (B1) is the hypothesis. It remains to establish clause (B2).

We split on the position $k$ along $\pi$.

Case 1 – end-position ($k = 0$ or $k = n$). By def def:unblockable_noncollider, clause ii., any unblockable non-collider on $\pi$ must be interior, i.e.\ $k \notin \{0, n\}$ (equivalently $1 \le k \le n - 1$). The hypothesis $k \in \{0, n\}$ of this case directly negates clause ii.\ of def def:unblockable_noncollider. Therefore $k$ is not an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$, establishing (B2). Hence $k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ in this case.

Case 2 – interior position ($1 \le k \le n - 1$). We argue by contradiction: suppose, toward contradiction, that $k$ is an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$ in the sense of def def:unblockable_noncollider. We will derive a non-trivial directed walk from $v_k$ to itself in $G$, contradicting def def-acylic.

Step 2.1 – the non-collider hypothesis yields an outgoing walk-edge of $v_k$. Since $k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$, def def:collider_noncollider, item i.\ gives $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \le 1$, where \[ \mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) = \Bigl|\Bigl\{\, i \in \{k - 1, k\} \,\Bigm|\, 0 \le i \le n - 1 \,\land\, a_i \text{ is an edge into } v_k \text{ in } G \,\Bigr\}\Bigr|. \] Since $1 \le k \le n - 1$, both indices $i \in \{k - 1, k\}$ are admissible (they satisfy $0 \le i \le n - 1$), so the index set defining $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$ is $\{k - 1, k\}$ and $|\{k - 1, k\}| = 2$. Hence $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k) \le 1$ means at least one of the two walk-incident edges $a_{k - 1}, a_k$ is not an edge into $v_k$ in the sense of def def-edge-relations, item ii.

We expand what “not an edge into $v_k$” forces on each walk-incident edge using the walk constraint of def def:walks, item i.\ (recalled in the statement above). For each admissible index $i \in \{k - 1, k\}$, the walk constraint at $i$ states \[ \bigl(a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \;\land\; a_i \in E \cup L\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl(a_i = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \;\land\; a_i \in E\bigr), \] so $a_i$ is one of three configurations:

  • [(C1)] $a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$ (a directed edge from $v_i$ to $v_{i+1}$);
  • [(C2)] $a_i = (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in L$ (a bidirected edge with the ordered-pair writing $(v_i, v_{i+1})$);
  • [(C3)] $a_i = (v_{i+1}, v_i) \in E$ (a directed edge from $v_{i+1}$ to $v_i$).

Applying the “edge into $v_k$” predicate of def def-edge-relations, item ii.\ ($a_i \in E$ with second coordinate $v_k$, or $a_i \in L$ with either coordinate $v_k$) to each configuration at slot $i \in \{k - 1, k\}$ (so the writing $a_i = (e_1, e_2)$ takes $e_1, e_2 \in \{v_i, v_{i+1}\}$ in some order, and $v_k \in \{v_i, v_{i+1}\}$ because $i \in \{k - 1, k\}$ means $\{v_i, v_{i+1}\}$ contains $v_k$):

  • For slot $i = k - 1$ ($v_i = v_{k - 1}, v_{i+1} = v_k$): \begin{itemize}
  • (C1) $a_{k-1} = (v_{k-1}, v_k) \in E$: into $v_k$ (the $E$-clause fires, $e_2 = v_k$).
  • (C2) $a_{k-1} = (v_{k-1}, v_k) \in L$: into $v_k$ (the $L$-clause fires, $e_2 = v_k$).
  • (C3) $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$: not into $v_k$ (the $E$-clause requires $e_2 = v_k$, but $e_2 = v_{k-1}$; and $L$-clause is excluded since $a_{k-1} \in E$). This is the only configuration at slot $k - 1$ for which $a_{k - 1}$ fails the “edge into $v_k$” predicate. \end{itemize}
  • For slot $i = k$ ($v_i = v_k, v_{i+1} = v_{k+1}$): \begin{itemize}
  • (C1) $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$: not into $v_k$ (the $E$-clause requires $e_2 = v_k$, but $e_2 = v_{k+1}$; and $L$-clause is excluded since $a_k \in E$). This is the only configuration at slot $k$ for which $a_k$ fails the “edge into $v_k$” predicate.
  • (C2) $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in L$: into $v_k$ (the $L$-clause fires, $e_1 = v_k$).
  • (C3) $a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \in E$: into $v_k$ (the $E$-clause fires, $e_2 = v_k$). \end{itemize}

In summary, an admissible slot $i \in \{k - 1, k\}$ has $a_i$ not an edge into $v_k$ iff either \[ i = k - 1 \;\text{and}\; a_{k - 1} = (v_k, v_{k - 1}) \in E, \qquad\text{or}\qquad i = k \;\text{and}\; a_k = (v_k, v_{k + 1}) \in E. \] By the previous paragraph, the non-collider hypothesis forces at least one such slot to occur. We capture this in a single statement: there exists a slot $i^* \in \{k - 1, k\}$ at which $a_{i^*}$ is an outgoing walk-edge of $v_k$ at position $k$ on $\pi$ in the sense of def def:unblockable_noncollider (the predicate “$a_i \in E \land e_1 = v_k$”), with the two sub-cases:

  • [(O1)] $i^* = k - 1$ with $a_{k - 1} = (v_k, v_{k - 1}) \in E$ – the other endpoint of $a_{k - 1}$ along $\pi$ is $v_{k - 1}$;
  • [(O2)] $i^* = k$ with $a_k = (v_k, v_{k + 1}) \in E$ – the other endpoint of $a_k$ along $\pi$ is $v_{k + 1}$.

(Both sub-cases may simultaneously hold – corresponding to the “fork” pattern – but for the argument below we just need at least one.)

Step 2.2 – the unblockable assumption forces the other endpoint into $\Sc^G(v_k)$. By the contradiction hypothesis, $k$ is unblockable on $\pi$, so clause iii.\ of def def:unblockable_noncollider fires: \[ \bigl(a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E \;\implies\; v_{k-1} \in \Sc^G(v_k)\bigr) \;\land\; \bigl(a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E \;\implies\; v_{k+1} \in \Sc^G(v_k)\bigr). \] Pick the slot $i^*$ from Step 2.1 and write $w$ for the other walk-endpoint at that slot: \[ w := v_{k - 1} \text{ if } i^* = k - 1, \qquad w := v_{k + 1} \text{ if } i^* = k. \] In either sub-case the antecedent of the corresponding conjunct fires (by the very definition of $i^*$ in Step 2.1), and we conclude \[ w \in \Sc^G(v_k), \] together with the directed-edge fact $(v_k, w) \in E$ recorded in Step 2.1.

Step 2.3 – $w \in \Sc^G(v_k)$ supplies a directed walk from $w$ to $v_k$. By def def_3_5, item vii., \[ \Sc^G(v_k) \;=\; \Anc^G(v_k) \,\cap\, \Desc^G(v_k), \] so $w \in \Sc^G(v_k)$ entails $w \in \Anc^G(v_k)$. By def def_3_5, item iv., $w \in \Anc^G(v_k)$ means there exists a directed walk $\rho$ from $w$ to $v_k$ in $G$ in the sense of def def:walks, item ii.\ – a tuple \[ \rho \;=\; (w = u_0, b_0, u_1, b_1, \dots, u_{m-1}, b_{m-1}, u_m = v_k) \] for some $m \ge 0$, with each $b_j = (u_j, u_{j+1}) \in E$ pointing forward along $\rho$ from $u_j$ to $u_{j+1}$.

Step 2.4 – concatenating the single edge $(v_k, w) \in E$ with $\rho$ yields a non-trivial directed walk from $v_k$ to itself. We assemble the directed walk \[ \widetilde{\rho} \;:=\; (v_k, \;(v_k, w), \;w = u_0, \;b_0, \;u_1, \;b_1, \;\dots, \;u_{m-1}, \;b_{m-1}, \;u_m = v_k). \] That is, prepend the node $v_k$ and the edge $(v_k, w) \in E$ to $\rho$. We verify that $\widetilde{\rho}$ is a directed walk from $v_k$ to itself in the sense of def def:walks, item ii.

  • Node and edge typing. The nodes of $\widetilde{\rho}$ are $v_k, w = u_0, u_1, \dots, u_m = v_k$, all in $J \cup V$ ($v_k$ by hypothesis of the walk $\pi$, the $u_j$ by the directed-walk $\rho$). The edges are $(v_k, w)$ followed by $b_0, b_1, \dots, b_{m-1}$, all in $E$ ($(v_k, w) \in E$ from Step 2.1, the $b_j \in E$ from $\rho$).
  • Length. The length of $\widetilde{\rho}$, counted by the number of edges, is $1 + m$, where $m \ge 0$. Hence $1 + m \ge 1$, so $\widetilde{\rho}$ is a non-trivial walk (its length is at least $1$, i.e.\ it has at least one edge).
  • Endpoints. The first node of $\widetilde{\rho}$ is $v_k$ (by construction), and the last node is $u_m = v_k$ (the terminus of $\rho$). So $\widetilde{\rho}$ starts and ends at $v_k$.
  • Directed-walk shape. Per def def:walks, item ii., a directed walk has all its edges of the form “forward” $(u_j, u_{j+1}) \in E$ at each successive slot, with arrowheads consistently pointing forward. The first edge of $\widetilde{\rho}$ is $(v_k, w) \in E$, fitting the slot writing $(\text{first node}, \text{second node}) = (v_k, w)$ in $E$; the subsequent edges $b_0, b_1, \dots, b_{m-1}$ are the directed-walk edges of $\rho$, hence fit the same forward-slot writing relative to $\rho$'s nodes; and the join between $(v_k, w)$ and $\rho$ at the node $w = u_0$ is consistent (the head of $(v_k, w)$ equals the start of $\rho$). All arrowheads point forward (from $v_k$ to $w$, then from $u_0$ through to $u_m$); no arrowhead points backward. Hence $\widetilde{\rho}$ has the directed-walk shape of def def:walks, item ii.

Therefore $\widetilde{\rho}$ is a directed walk from $v_k$ to $v_k$ in $G$ of length at least $1$ – equivalently, a non-trivial directed walk from $v_k$ to itself in $G$.

Step 2.5 – contradiction with acyclicity. By def def-acylic, $G$ acyclic asserts that no non-trivial directed walk from any node to itself exists in $G$. But Step 2.4 has constructed exactly such a walk $\widetilde{\rho}$ at the node $v_k \in J \cup V$. Contradiction.

Hence the assumption that $k$ is an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$ is false. So $k$ is not an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$, establishing (B2) in the interior case.

In both cases (end-position and interior), the hypothesis “$k$ is a non-collider on $\pi$” implies $k$ is not an unblockable non-collider on $\pi$. Combining with (B1), $k$ is a blockable non-collider on $\pi$ in the sense of def def:unblockable_noncollider.

lean_blocks.explanation: This is a `variable` declaration: Lean auto-binds `Node : Type*` and the instance `[DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments to every subsequent declaration in the surrounding `CDMG` namespace, sparing us from rewriting the same parameters on `acyclic_non_colliders_blockable`. The `Type*` notation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, so callers can instantiate it at any universe level. The `DecidableEq Node` assumption is load-bearing because the per-position predicates `IsNonCollider` (from `def_3_15`) and `IsBlockableNonCollider` (from `def_3_16`) both require it — their internal `Finset.filter` over the walk's incident edges needs decidable equality on `Node` to compute the arrowhead count $\mathrm{ah}_\pi(k)$. Stronger structure on `Node` (e.g.\ `Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not required here and is deferred to downstream consumers that actually use it. Same convention as the rest of `Chapter3_GraphTheory`'s files.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Auto-binds `Node` + `DecidableEq Node` as implicits; decidability is required by `IsNonCollider`/`IsBlockableNonCollider`'s internal `Finset` machinery.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.code:
theorem acyclic_non_colliders_blockable
    (G : CDMG Node) (hG : G.IsAcyclic)
    {u v : Node} (π : Walk G u v) (k : ℕ) :
    π.IsNonCollider k → π.IsBlockableNonCollider k
lean_blocks.explanation: This block is the statement head of the LN remark: in an acyclic CDMG, every non-collider position on every walk is blockable. The binder list reads `(G : CDMG Node) (hG : G.IsAcyclic) {u v : Node} (π : Walk G u v) (k : ℕ)`, mirroring the LN's layered "let $G$ be a CDMG, suppose $G$ is acyclic, then for every walk $\pi$ … every position $k$". CDMG-hood is structural (typed `G : CDMG Node`), while acyclicity is the propositional `def_3_6` predicate `G.IsAcyclic`. The endpoints `{u v}` are implicit because `Walk G u v` already carries them. The conclusion `π.IsNonCollider k → π.IsBlockableNonCollider k` reuses the per-position predicates of `def_3_15` and `def_3_16` verbatim; by `def_3_16`, `IsBlockableNonCollider` unfolds to `IsNonCollider ∧ ¬ IsUnblockableNonCollider`, so the conclusion definitionally re-asserts the antecedent in its first conjunct. No `k ≤ π.length` side condition appears: `IsNonCollider`'s own in-range conjunct makes the antecedent `False` at out-of-range positions, rendering the implication vacuous and the LN's $k \in \{0, \dots, n\}$ scope implicit. The proof body, omitted from this surface block, executes the TeX proof's Step 2.1–2.5 cascade.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- ref: claim_3_20 — statement head; the proof body lives below the wrapped surface.
theorem acyclic_non_colliders_blockable
    -- `G` typed as a CDMG (structural CDMG-hood); `hG` carries `def_3_6`'s acyclicity proposition.
    (G : CDMG Node) (hG : G.IsAcyclic)
    -- Endpoints `{u v}` implicit — recoverable from `Walk G u v`'s type; `π` and `k` explicit per `def_3_16`'s binder shape.
    {u v : Node} (π : Walk G u v) (k : ℕ) :
    -- LN-faithful implication: `def_3_15`'s `IsNonCollider` ⟹ `def_3_16`'s `IsBlockableNonCollider`; `IsNonCollider`'s in-range conjunct makes out-of-range `k` vacuous.
    π.IsNonCollider k → π.IsBlockableNonCollider k

addition_to_the_LN: [acyclic_does_not_imply_directed_in_text] In this remark $G$ is a CDMG, as fixed by the surrounding context; the hypothesis "$G$ is acyclic" is to be read as acyclicity in the CDMG sense, so the edge orientations needed to define (non-)colliders are already part of the ambient structure.
design_choices:
**The CDMG is typed; acyclicity is propositional.** The LN reads "let $G$ be a CDMG and suppose $G$ is acyclic". The formalization splits these asymmetrically: CDMG-hood is enforced at the type level by `G : CDMG Node` (no separate hypothesis), while acyclicity is carried by the opt-in propositional hypothesis `hG : G.IsAcyclic` using `def_3_6`'s predicate verbatim. This mirrors how the rest of §3.3 composes hypotheses — most per-position predicates take a bare `CDMG` and do *not* assume acyclicity, so making acyclicity an explicit `Prop`-level binder keeps the theorem maximally compatible with the broader section. Bundling both into a typeclass `[AcyclicCDMG Node G]` or into a structured sub-type was rejected because it would force every downstream consumer of the per-position predicates to re-derive the bare CDMG view.

**Conclusion as an implication, not as a conjunction.** The LN says "(non-collider) $\implies$ (blockable non-collider)". The formalization transports this implication-in-conclusion shape word-for-word. A leaner variant `IsNonCollider k → ¬ IsUnblockableNonCollider k` would drop the definitional redundancy (since `IsBlockableNonCollider` already contains `IsNonCollider` as a conjunct) but force every downstream consumer to re-assemble the LN-named predicate by hand. Keeping the conclusion as `IsBlockableNonCollider` lets a caller with the non-collider hypothesis in scope obtain the LN-named predicate in a single step, without unfolding `def_3_16`.

**Per-walk-per-position quantification at the binder level.** The two per-position predicates `IsNonCollider` and `IsBlockableNonCollider` are both indexed by `(p, k)`. The theorem mirrors this verbatim: the binders introduce `(π, k)` directly, and the conclusion is the per-position implication. The alternative `∀ π k, π.IsNonCollider k → π.IsBlockableNonCollider k` form, pushed *inside* the conclusion, would be logically equivalent but force consumers to apply the explicit quantifier before reading off the implication — breaking the chapter-wide binder-level convention.

**Implicit walk endpoints `{u v : Node}`.** Since `Walk G u v` carries its source and target in its type, the endpoints are always recoverable from any walk and never need to be named at the call site. Making them implicit reproduces the binder shape of `IsBlockableNonCollider` and keeps the theorem ergonomically compatible with dot-notation.

**No explicit position bound.** The LN scopes $k \in \{0, \dots, n\}$; the formalization carries this implicitly. `IsNonCollider`'s own in-range conjunct already enforces $k \le \pi.\text{length}$, so out-of-range positions make the antecedent `False` and the implication holds vacuously. An explicit `k ≤ π.length` hypothesis would be redundant.
